# Tzais (Geonim)

[Back to all zmanim](index.md)

## Tzais Geonim 3 Point 7 Degrees

This zman is deprecated.

Tzais, or nightfall, according to an opinion of the Geonim, set when the sun is 3.7° below the geometric zenith. This corresponds to 13.5 minutes after sunset in Jerusalem around the [equinox / equilux](https://kosherjava.com/2022/01/12/equinox-vs-equilux-zmanim-calculations/).

It is based on 3/4 of a [mil](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biblical_and_Talmudic_units_of_measurement) at 18 minutes per mil, which is 13.5 minutes after sunset; at that point the sun is 3.7° below the geometric zenith in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux.

This calculation does not include the 26.46 seconds for walking 49 amos, described as the heref ayin of bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi, at the pace of an 18-minute mil.

Rabbi Yedidya Manet in [Zmanei HaHalacha Lema'aseh](https://www.nli.org.il/en/books/NNL_ALEPH002542826/NLI), 4th edition part 2, pages 22 and 24, lists 3.65°, which is described as appearing to be a drop too early.

??? info "Technical details"
    Source method: `ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim3Point7Degrees`

    Technical reference: `com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim3Point7Degrees`

## Tzais Geonim 3 Point 8 Degrees

This zman is deprecated.

Tzais, or nightfall, according to an opinion of the Geonim, set when the sun is 3.8° below the geometric zenith. This corresponds to 14 minutes after sunset in Jerusalem around the [equinox / equilux](https://kosherjava.com/2022/01/12/equinox-vs-equilux-zmanim-calculations/).

It is based on 3/4 of a [mil](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biblical_and_Talmudic_units_of_measurement) at 18 minutes per mil, or 13.5 minutes after sunset, plus 30 seconds for the time to walk 49 amos, described as the heref ayin of bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi. This totals 14 minutes after sunset; at that point the sun is 3.8° below the geometric zenith in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux.

At the pace of an 18-minute mil, 49 amos would take 26.46 seconds, rounded to 30 seconds.

??? info "Technical details"
    Source method: `ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim3Point8Degrees`

    Technical reference: `com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim3Point8Degrees`

## Tzais Geonim 4 Point 42 Degrees

This zman is deprecated.

Tzais, or nightfall, according to an opinion of the Geonim, set when the sun is 4.42° below the western horizon. It is described as a very early zman.

It is based on 3/4 of a [mil](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biblical_and_Talmudic_units_of_measurement) using a 22.5-minute mil, which is 16 7/8 minutes after sunset.

This very early zman should not be relied on without Rabbinical guidance.

This calculation does not include the 33.07 seconds for walking 49 amos, described as the heref ayin of bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi, at the pace of a 22.5-minute mil.

Rabbi Yedidya Manet in [Zmanei HaHalacha Lema'aseh](https://www.nli.org.il/en/books/NNL_ALEPH002542826/NLI), 4th edition part 2, pages 22 and 24, lists 4.37°, which is described as appearing to be a drop too early.

In northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, this time may not be available when the sun does not reach low enough below the horizon.

??? info "Technical details"
    Source method: `ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim4Point42Degrees`

    Technical reference: `com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim4Point42Degrees`

## Tzais Geonim 4 Point 66 Degrees

This zman is deprecated.

Tzais, or nightfall, according to an opinion of the Geonim, set when the sun is 4.66° below the western horizon. It is described as a very early zman.

It is based on 3/4 of a [mil](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biblical_and_Talmudic_units_of_measurement) using a 24-minute mil, which is 18 minutes after sunset.

This very early zman should not be relied on without Rabbinical guidance.

This calculation does not include the 35.28 seconds for walking 49 amos, described as the heref ayin of bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi, at the pace of a 24-minute mil.

In northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, this time may not be available when the sun does not reach low enough below the horizon.

## Tzais Geonim 4 Point 8 Degrees

Tzais, or nightfall, according to an opinion of the Geonim, set when the sun is 4.8° below the western horizon. It is described as an early zman for tzais.

It is based on 3/4 of a 24-minute [mil](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biblical_and_Talmudic_units_of_measurement), plus 0.6 minutes for the time to walk 49 amos for bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi, for a total of 18.6 minutes after sunset. This corresponds to the sun being 4.8° below the western horizon.

This is based on [Rav Yechiel Michel Shlezinger's](https://he.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D7%99%D7%97%D7%99%D7%90%D7%9C_%D7%9E%D7%99%D7%9B%D7%9C_%D7%A9%D7%9C%D7%96%D7%99%D7%A0%D7%92%D7%A8) sefer [Aizehu Bain Hashmashos](https://www.nli.org.il/he/books/NNL_ALEPH997010042055805171/NLI) and [Rabbi Yehuda (Leo) Levi's](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yehuda_(Leo)_Levi) calculations in [Zmanei Hayom BaHalacha](https://www.nli.org.il/en/items/NNL_ALEPH990022548970205171/NLI), p. 37.

At this point, 3 medium-sized stars are visible to a non-expert with good vision with effort. An expert who knows where to look can see 3 medium stars as early as 15 minutes after sunset.

Hazmanim Bahalacha is cited as explaining that medium-sized stars would be visible to an expert as early as 14 minutes after sunset, based on 13.5 minutes for 3/4 of an 18-minute mil plus 0.5 minutes for bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi.

This early zman for tzais should not be relied on without Rabbinical guidance.

In northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, this time may not be available when the sun does not reach low enough below the horizon.

??? info "Technical details"
    Source method: `ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim4Point8Degrees`

    Technical reference: `com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim4Point8Degrees`

## Tzais Geonim 5 Point 95 Degrees

Tzais, or nightfall, according to an opinion of the Geonim, set when the sun is 5.95° below the geometric zenith. This is tied to the sun’s position 24 minutes after sunset in Jerusalem around the [equinox / equilux](https://kosherjava.com/2022/01/12/equinox-vs-equilux-zmanim-calculations/).

The 24 minutes are based on the Baal Hatanya’s calculation of 18 minutes, which is 3/4 of a 24-minute mil, plus 4 minutes for shkiah amitis, plus 2 minutes for bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi.

Sources cited include Hazmanim Bahalacha vol. II, ch. 50 no. 5, pp. 512–513, ch. 47, and Yisrael Vehazmanim [Vol III, ch. 13, no. 53, p. 1026](https://hebrewbooks.org/pdfpager.aspx?req=9764&st=&pgnum=266).

Among the sources mentioned for this zman are [Rabbi Yehuda (Leo) Levi's](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yehuda_(Leo)_Levi) calculations in Jewish Chrononomy and other sources.

Calculations are described as showing that the time is closer to 5.93° and was seemingly rounded to 5.95°.

Chabad calendars usually use a 6°-based time built on this same calculation.

Rabbi Yedidya Manet in [Zmanei HaHalacha Lema'aseh](https://www.nli.org.il/en/books/NNL_ALEPH002542826/NLI), 4th edition part 2, pages 22 and 24, lists 5.88°, which is described as appearing to be a drop too early.

In northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, this time may not be available when the sun does not reach low enough below the horizon.

??? info "Technical details"
    Source method: `ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim5Point95Degrees`

    Technical reference: `com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim5Point95Degrees`

## Tzais Geonim 6 Point 45 Degrees

Tzais, or nightfall, according to the opinion of the Geonim as calculated by [Rabbi Yechiel Michel Tucazinsky](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yechiel_Michel_Tucazinsky), set when the sun is 6.45° below the geometric zenith. It is described as the commonly used zman in Israel.

It is based on the sun’s position no later than 31 minutes after sea-level sunset in Jerusalem. At the summer solstice, calculations show that 30.75 minutes after shkiah corresponds to 6.45° below the geometric zenith. At the equinox, 6.45° is described as 26.5 minutes after shkiah around the [equinox / equilux](https://kosherjava.com/2022/01/12/equinox-vs-equilux-zmanim-calculations/).

The Birur Halacha is cited as showing that Rav Tucazinsky’s sunset calculations, listed as 28 minutes in this case, were about 3 minutes later than reality.

This calculation is found in the [Birur Halacha Yoreh Deah 262](https://hebrewbooks.org/pdfpager.aspx?req=50536&st=&pgnum=51).

It is also used in the [Luach Itim Lebinah](https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/243303103).

This differs from the 6.1° / 6.2° calculation for Rabbi Tucazinsky’s time as calculated by Hazmanim Bahalacha vol. II, chapter 50:7, p. 515.

In northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, this time may not be available when the sun does not reach low enough below the horizon.

??? info "Technical details"
    Source method: `ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim6Point45Degrees`

    Technical reference: `com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim6Point45Degrees`

## Tzais Geonim 7 Point 083 Degrees

Tzais, or nightfall, according to an opinion of the Geonim, set when the sun is 7.083°, also written as 7° 5′, below the western horizon.

It is based on Dr. Baruch Berthold Cohn’s observation of 3 medium-sized stars in his luach [Tabellen enthaltend die Zeitangaben für den Beginn der Nacht und des Tages für die Breitengrade + 66 bis -38](https://sammlungen.ub.uni-frankfurt.de/freimann/content/titleinfo/983088), published in Strasbourg in 1899.

This calendar was very popular in Europe, and many other calendars based their tzais time on it.

[Rav Dovid Tzvi Hoffman](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/David_Zvi_Hoffmann), in [שו״ת מלמד להועיל](https://hebrewbooks.org/1053), in an exchange of letters with Baruch Cohn in [Orach Chaim 30](https://hebrewbooks.org/pdfpager.aspx?req=1053&st=&pgnum=37), agreed to this zman, as did the שו״ת בני ציון and the [תנובת שדה](https://hebrewbooks.org/67373).

It is very close to the time of the [מקור חסד](https://hebrewbooks.org/22044) of the Sefer chasidim.

It is close to, but not exactly, the sun’s position 30 minutes after sunset in Jerusalem around the [equinox / equilux](https://kosherjava.com/2022/01/12/equinox-vs-equilux-zmanim-calculations/). The actual position of the sun 30 minutes after sunset in Jerusalem is stated as 7.205° at the equilux and 7.199° at the equinox.

Hazmanim Bahalacha vol. 2, pages 520–521 is cited for more details.

In northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, this time may not be available when the sun does not reach low enough below the horizon.

??? info "Technical details"
    Source method: `ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim7Point083Degrees`

    Technical reference: `com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim7Point083Degrees`

## Tzais Geonim 7 Point 67 Degrees

Tzais, or nightfall, according to the Geonim, based on 45 minutes after sunset during the summer solstice in New York, when twilight is longest. Sources cited for this zman include [Igros Moshe Even Haezer 4, Ch. 4](https://hebrewbooks.org/pdfpager.aspx?req=921&pgnum=149), Rabbi Heber's [Shaarei Zmanim](https://hebrewbooks.org/53000), [chapter 10 (page 87)](https://hebrewbooks.org/pdfpager.aspx?req=53055&pgnum=101), [chapter 12 (page 108)](https://hebrewbooks.org/pdfpager.aspx?req=53055&pgnum=122), Rabbi Simcha Bunim Cohen's The radiance of Shabbos, [Divrei Shalom Vol. III, chapter 75](https://hebrewbooks.org/pdfpager.aspx?req=1927&pgnum=90), and [Bais Av"i Vol. III, chapter 117](https://hebrewbooks.org/pdfpager.aspx?req=892&pgnum=431).

Calculated as the time when the sun is 7.67° below sea level, corresponding to the sun’s position 45 minutes after sunset during the summer solstice in New York.

This zman depends on the level of light; Rabbi Yaakov Shakow presented this degree-based calculation to [Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shmuel_Kamenetsky), who agreed to it.

The time may not be available in northern or southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle or north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

??? info "Technical details"
    Source method: `ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim7Point67Degrees`

    Technical reference: `com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim7Point67Degrees`

## Tzais Geonim 9 Point 3 Degrees

Tzais, or nightfall, based on the calculations used in the [Luach Itim Lebinah](https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/243303103) as the stringent time for tzais.

Calculated as the time when the sun is 9.3° below sea level, below the western horizon.

The time may not be available in northern or southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle or north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

??? info "Technical details"
    Source method: `ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim9Point3Degrees`

    Technical reference: `com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim9Point3Degrees`

## Tzais Geonim 9 Point 75 Degrees

Tzais, or nightfall, according to the Geonim, based on 60 minutes after sunset [around the equinox / equilux](https://kosherjava.com/2022/01/12/equinox-vs-equilux-zmanim-calculations/), the day that a solar hour is 60 minutes in New York. This is the opinion of [Rabbi Eliyahu Henkin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yosef_Eliyahu_Henkin) and also follows the opinion of [Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shmuel_Kamenetsky).

Calculated as the time when the sun is 9.75° below sea level, corresponding to the sun’s position 60 minutes after sunset around the equinox or equilux in New York.

Rabbi Yaakov Shakow presented these degree-based times to Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky, who agreed to them.

The time may not be available in northern or southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle or north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

??? info "Technical details"
    Source method: `ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim9Point75Degrees`

    Technical reference: `com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim9Point75Degrees`

## Tzais Geonim 8 Point 5 Degrees

Tzais, or nightfall, when the sun is 8.5° below the geometric horizon after sunset. Rabbi Meir Posen in the [Ohr Meir](https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/29283612) calculated this as the time when 3 small stars are visible, which is later than the required 3 medium stars.

Calculated as the time when the sun is 8.5° below the geometric zenith. This is based on the sun’s position below the horizon 36 minutes after sunset in Jerusalem [around the equinox / equilux](https://kosherjava.com/2022/01/12/equinox-vs-equilux-zmanim-calculations/).

The time may not be available in northern or southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle or north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

??? info "Technical details"
    Source method: `ZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim8Point5Degrees`

    Technical reference: `com.kosherjava.zmanim.ZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim8Point5Degrees`
